title: Ginger nuts
subtitle: Crunchy ginger snap biscuits
excerpt: Classic crunchy ginger biscuits made without egg — just butter, golden syrup, flour, sugar, and plenty of ground ginger, plus bicarbonate of soda for the slight puff and crinkle on the surface. Leave on the tray to cool and firm before moving. Makes twenty-four.
sourceNotes: Ginger biscuits are documented in British domestic cookbooks from the seventeenth century onward. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: ginger nuts are documented in the biscuit chapter as a hard, crunchy biscuit made with flour, sugar, butter, and ground ginger, with golden syrup providing colour and snap. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: ginger biscuits made with golden syrup and ground ginger are documented as a standard British domestic preparation.

recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe and bake in two batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in an airtight tin for up to 2 weeks. The biscuits crisp further over the first day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. Leave on the tray for 10 minutes after baking — the biscuits are soft when hot and firm to a snap as they cool

glossaryTerms.definition: A biscuit baked until completely dry and hard throughout, with no soft centre. The snap refers to the clean break when the biscuit is broken in two — an indication that all the moisture has been driven off. To achieve a snap, leave the biscuits on the tray to cool completely rather than moving them immediately to a rack; moving them while warm interrupts the drying-out process. If the biscuits are still soft when cold, return them to the oven for 5 minutes more.

content.text: Ginger nuts should snap, not bend. They come out of the oven soft — that is correct. They firm as they cool on the tray. Do not move them to a rack while warm or they will not reach their full crunch. The ginger flavour should be sharp; adjust the quantity to taste.

Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Whisk together the {{plain-flour}}, {{ginger-ground}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{caster-sugar}} in a large bowl. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{golden-syrup}} together in a small saucepan over low heat, then pour into the dry ingredients and stir until a firm dough forms. Roll into balls of about 12 g each (a rounded teaspoon) and space them 4 cm apart on the trays. Flatten slightly. Bake for 12–14 minutes until the surface is cracked and dry. These are
: leave them on the tray for 10 minutes to firm before moving to a rack.
